Tutorial: Week 7 — Projections and Left/Right Inverses + Diagonal Cross-Filling Review ​

For: Teaching Assistants conducting tutorial sessions (Apr 9, 2026) Related Lectures: Lecture 8: Cross-Filling Projections, Lecture 9: Compatible Projections, Lecture 10: Constructing ProjectionsDuration: 50 minutes Context: This is the last tutorial before the midterm (Apr 13). The goal is to (1) connect projections with left/right inverses, and (2) review diagonal cross-filling as a computational tool. Both topics integrate key ideas from Chapters 1–3.

Part A: Projections and Left/Right Inverses (30 min) ​

Learning Goal ​

Students understand that every projection arises from a left inverse, and conversely every left inverse produces a projection. Different left inverses of the same matrix give different projections onto the same column space — the choice of left inverse is the choice of "sunlight direction."

Problem 1: From Left Inverse to Projection (10 min) ​

Let U=(100111) (3×2, rank 2).

(a) Verify that L1=(100010) is a left inverse of U.

Solution

L1U=(100010)(100111)=(1001)=I2 ✓

(b) Compute P1=UL1 and verify that P12=P1.

SolutionP1=UL1=(100111)(100010)=(100010110)

P12=(UL1)(UL1)=U(L1U)L1=U⋅I2⋅L1=UL1=P1 ✓

Key point: The proof uses only L1U=I — it works for any left inverse!

(c) What are Col(P1) and Null(P1)?

Solution

Col(P1)=Col(U)=span{(101),(011)} (since P1=UL1, every column of P1 is in Col(U); and P1U=U(L1U)=U, so Col(U)⊆Col(P1)).

Null(P1)=Null(L1)=span{(001)} (since P1x=0⟺UL1x=0⟺L1x=0 because U is left cancelable).

(d) Now take a different left inverse: L2=(0−11−101). Verify L2U=I2, then compute P2=UL2.

Solution

L2U=(0−11−101)(100111)=(0+0+10−1+1−1+0+10+0+1)=I2 ✓

P2=UL2=(100111)(0−11−101)=(0−11−101−1−12)

(e) Compare P1 and P2: same column space? Same null space?

Solution

Same column space: Col(P1)=Col(P2)=Col(U) (both project onto the same subspace).

Different null spaces:

  • Null(P1)=Null(L1)=span{(001)}
  • Null(P2)=Null(L2)=span{(111)}

Geometric interpretation: Both projections land on the same "floor" (Col(U)), but the "sunlight" comes from different directions. The sunlight direction = Null(L) — this is what varies with the choice of left inverse.

Attention

The Key Correspondence

Left inverses of U⟷Projections onto Col(U)
  • Every left inverse L gives a projection P=UL onto Col(U)
  • The null space of L determines the sunlight direction (= Null(P))
  • Different left inverses → same floor, different sunlight → different projections

Problem 2: From Projection to Left Inverse (8 min) ​

This reverses the direction: given a projection, extract a left inverse.

Let P be a projection with Col(P)=Col(U) where U is m×r with rank r.

(a) Cross-fill P=UV with VU=Ir (Lecture 8). Show that V is a left inverse of U.

Solution

VU=Ir is exactly the definition of "V is a left inverse of U." ✓

And indeed P=UV gives P=U⋅V, which is exactly the P=UL construction from Problem 1.

(b) What does this say about the relationship between the construction formula P=B(AB)−1A from Lecture 10 and left inverses?

Solution

In the formula P=B(AB)−1A:

  • B plays the role of U (columns spanning the floor)
  • (AB)−1A plays the role of L (a left inverse of B)

Verify: (AB)−1A⋅B=(AB)−1(AB)=Ir ✓

So the construction formula is just: choose a left inverse of B, then multiply. The matrix A determines which left inverse — and thus which sunlight direction (Null(A)).

Problem 3: The Orthogonal Choice (12 min) ​

Among all left inverses of U, one is special: U+=(UTU)−1UT.

(a) Verify U+ is a left inverse of U (for the same U=(100111)).

Solution

UTU=(101011)(100111)=(2112)

(UTU)−1=13(2−1−12)

U+=13(2−1−12)(101011)=13(2−11−121)

Check: U+U=13(2+0+10−1+1−1+0+10+2+1)=I2 ✓

(b) Compute P⊥=UU+ and verify that P⊥=P⊥T (i.e., this projection is orthogonal).

SolutionP⊥=UU+=(100111)⋅13(2−11−121)=13(2−11−121112)

P⊥T=13(2−11−121112)=P⊥ ✓ (symmetric!)

This is because Null(U+)=Null(UT)=Col(U)⊥ — the sunlight direction is perpendicular to the floor.

(c) Compare the three projections P1, P2, P⊥:

P1 (from L1)P2 (from L2)P⊥ (from U+)
Column spaceCol(U)Col(U)Col(U)
Null spacespan{(001)}span{(111)}span{(11−1)}
Symmetric?NoNoYes
Null space of P⊥

Null(P⊥)=Null(UT)=Null(101011).

Solve: x1+x3=0, x2+x3=0. Free variable x3=t: Null(UT)=span{(11−1)}.

Check orthogonality: (11−1)T(101)=1+0−1=0 ✓, (11−1)T(011)=0+1−1=0 ✓

The sunlight is perpendicular to the floor — this is why P⊥ is the orthogonal projection.

Part B: Diagonal Cross-Filling Review (20 min) ​

Learning Goal ​

Students can carry out diagonal cross-filling by hand and connect it to inner diagonal cross-filling of (BTB)−1 for finding orthogonal bases.

Problem 4: Diagonal Cross-Filling Warm-Up (8 min) ​

Diagonally cross-fill the following symmetric matrix:

G=(2112)

(a) Cross-fill at diagonal pivot (1,1): pivot value = 2.

Solution

Column 1: (21), Row 1: (21).

R1=12(21)(21)=(2111/2)

Remainder: G−R1=(0003/2)=R2.

(b) Verify: R1 and R2 are both rank-1 and both symmetric.

Solution

R1=12(21)(21): rank 1 ✓, symmetric ✓ (outer product of a vector with itself).

R2=32(01)(01): rank 1 ✓, symmetric ✓.

Why is symmetry preserved? Diagonal pivots produce rank-1 pieces of the form 1akkccT (column = row transposed), so each piece is automatically symmetric.

Problem 5: Inner Diagonal Cross-Filling for Orthogonal Basis (12 min) ​

Let B=(110110) (full column rank). Find an orthogonal basis for Col(B) using inner diagonal cross-filling of (BTB)−1.

(a) Compute BTB and (BTB)−1.

SolutionBTB=(101110)(110110)=(2112)

det(BTB)=4−1=3.

(BTB)−1=13(2−1−12)

(b) Diagonally cross-fill (BTB)−1. At each step, read off the pivot column dj.

Solution

Step 1: Pivot at (1,1), value 23.

Column 1: d1=13(2−1), proportional to (2−1).

Q1=12/3⋅13(2−1)⋅13(2−1)=16(4−2−21)

Remainder: (BTB)−1−Q1=13(2−1−12)−16(4−2−21)=16(0003)=Q2

Step 2: d2∝(01).

(c) Compute v1=Bd1 and v2=Bd2.

Solutionv1=B(2−1)=(110110)(2−1)=(1−12)v2=B(01)=(110)

(d) Verify: v1⊥v2 and both lie in Col(B).

Solution

v1Tv2=(1)(1)+(−1)(1)+(2)(0)=0 ✓

Both are of the form Bd, so they lie in Col(B) by construction. ✓

Result: {(1−12),(110)} is an orthogonal basis for Col(B).

Remark

Why Does This Work?

The diagonal cross-filling (BTB)−1=Q1+Q2 produces rank-1 pieces Rj=BQjBT that are rank-1 orthogonal projections forming a compatible family (R1R2=0). Compatible orthogonal projections have mutually orthogonal column spaces. The vectors Bdj span these column spaces — hence they are automatically orthogonal.

This is the cross-filling alternative to Gram-Schmidt!

TA Notes ​

Time allocation ​

PartProblemSuggested TimePriority
AProblem 1 (left inverse → projection)10 minCore — walk through (a)–(c), let students compute (d)–(e)
AProblem 2 (projection → left inverse)8 minCore — the conceptual reverse direction
AProblem 3 (orthogonal choice)12 minCore — (a)–(b) computation, (c) comparison table is the payoff
BProblem 4 (diagonal cross-filling warm-up)8 minReview — quick computation practice
BProblem 5 (inner diagonal cross-filling)12 minCore — the main computational technique to practice

Suggested flow ​

  1. Start with Problem 1(a)–(c): Set up the key idea — left inverse gives projection. Let students compute P1=UL1 and verify P12=P1. Emphasize that the proof only uses LU=I.
  2. Problem 1(d)–(e): Students compute P2 themselves, then compare. The punchline: same floor, different sunlight.
  3. Problem 2: Brief conceptual discussion connecting back to the construction formula from Lecture 10. This can be presented quickly.
  4. Problem 3: Compute U+ and P⊥. The comparison table in (c) is the key takeaway — emphasize "orthogonal = symmetric = sunlight ⊥ floor."
  5. Problem 4: Quick warm-up to recall diagonal cross-filling mechanics. Can be done on the board in 3 minutes.
  6. Problem 5: The main computational exercise. Let students work through (a)–(c) in pairs, verify (d) together. Emphasize: no Gram-Schmidt needed.

Common mistakes ​

  • Confusing left and right inverses: LU=I (left inverse) vs UR=I (right inverse). Left inverse for thin full-rank, right inverse for fat full-rank.
  • Thinking left inverse is unique: It's not! The choice of null space determines which left inverse. The one-to-one correspondence with complements is the key insight.
  • Forgetting why UL is a projection: Students may verify P2=P by brute force instead of the clean argument P2=U(LU)L=UIL=UL=P. Emphasize the algebraic shortcut.
  • Diagonal cross-filling pivot errors: Students sometimes forget to divide by the pivot value when forming the rank-1 piece. Remind them: Rk=1akk⋅(column)⋅(row).
